sentences.text: Kasper geht in den dunklen Wald.
sentences.translation: Kasper goes into the dark forest.

words.grammar: gehen · present tense · 3rd person singular
in + accusative (movement into a place) · der Wald → den Wald · dunklen = adjective with weak ending after definite article
location adverb · sentence-opening position triggers inversion: wohnt comes before eine Hexe
wohnen · present tense · 3rd person singular
die Hexe · feminine · nominative · subject · alte = adjective with strong ending after indefinite article

sentences.text: Die Hexe sieht Kasper und ruft: Komm her, kleiner Mann!
sentences.translation: The witch sees Kasper and shouts: Come here, little man!

words.grammar: sehen · present tense · 3rd person singular · vowel change: e → ie
herkommen · separable verb · imperative du-form · her goes to end: 'Komm her!' · her = toward the speaker
der Mann · masculine · nominative · used as direct address (Anrede) · kleiner = strong adjective ending (no article)

sentences.text: Kasper antwortet: Nein! Ich kenne dich, du böse Hexe!
sentences.translation: Kasper answers: No! I know you, you wicked witch!

words.grammar: kennen · present tense · 1st person singular · kennen = to know someone/something (not wissen, which is for facts)
du → dich · accusative · direct object of kennen · informal singular
used as direct address · böse = strong adjective ending (no article before it) · böse = wicked / evil / naughty

sentences.text: Die Hexe sagt: Ich will dich in einen Frosch verwandeln!
sentences.translation: The witch says: I want to turn you into a frog!

words.grammar: wollen · modal verb · present tense · 1st person singular · modal + infinitive at end of clause
du → dich · accusative · direct object of verwandeln
in + accusative (transformation into something) · der Frosch → einen Frosch · masculine accusative
infinitive · sent to end of clause by modal verb wollen · jemanden in etwas verwandeln = to turn someone into something

sentences.text: Kasper lacht laut und ruft die Kinder: Habt ihr Angst vor der Hexe?
sentences.translation: Kasper laughs loudly and calls to the children: Are you afraid of the witch?

words.grammar: lachen · present tense · 3rd person singular

words.definition: are you afraid / do you have fear
words.grammar: Angst haben = to be afraid (lit. to have fear) · question inversion: habt before ihr · 2nd person plural
Angst vor + dative · fixed expression · vor der Hexe = dative after vor in this idiomatic use

sentences.text: Die Kinder rufen: Nein! Wir haben keine Angst!
sentences.translation: The children shout: No! We are not afraid!

words.grammar: haben · present tense · 1st person plural
kein + Angst · kein negates nouns · keine = feminine form (die Angst) · contrast: 'nicht' negates verbs/adjectives, 'kein' negates nouns

sentences.text: Kasper nimmt einen großen Besen und jagt die Hexe weg.
sentences.translation: Kasper takes a big broom and chases the witch away.

words.grammar: nehmen · present tense · 3rd person singular · vowel change: e → i
der Besen · masculine · accusative · einen = indefinite article accusative · großen = weak adjective ending after einen
wegjagen · separable verb · present tense · 3rd person singular · weg goes to end of clause

sentences.text: Die Hexe ruft: Ich komme wieder, weil ich dich nicht vergesse!
sentences.translation: The witch shouts: I will come back, because I won't forget you!

words.grammar: wiederkommen · separable verb · present tense · 1st person singular · wieder goes to end of clause
subordinating conjunction · sends the verb to the END of its clause: 'weil ich dich nicht vergesse' (not: 'weil ich vergesse dich nicht')
vergessen · present tense · 1st person singular · pushed to end of clause by weil

sentences.text: Aber Kasper ruft zurück: Du wirst mich nicht finden!
sentences.translation: But Kasper shouts back: You will not find me!

words.grammar: zurückrufen · separable verb · present tense · 3rd person singular · zurück goes to end
werden · present tense · 2nd person singular · used with infinitive to express future: du wirst + finden = you will find
ich → mich · accusative · direct object of finden
infinitive · sent to end of clause by werden (future construction)

sentences.text: Kasper versteckt sich hinter einem großen Baum und wartet.
sentences.translation: Kasper hides behind a big tree and waits.

words.grammar: sich verstecken · reflexive verb · present tense · 3rd person singular · sich = reflexive pronoun (accusative)
hinter + dative (static location, not movement) · einem Baum · großen = weak adjective ending after einem
warten · present tense · 3rd person singular

sentences.text: Die Hexe kommt nicht zurück, und alle sind froh.
sentences.translation: The witch does not come back, and everyone is happy.

words.grammar: zurückkommen · separable · negated with nicht · nicht sits before the separable prefix: 'kommt nicht zurück'
alle · plural · nominative · subject of sind · used without a noun: alle = everyone present
predicate adjective after sein · no ending needed: 'alle sind froh' (contrast with attributive: 'frohe Kinder')

grammarPoints.rule: Modal verb 'wollen' + infinitive at end of clause
grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Ich will dich in einen Frosch verwandeln!
grammarPoints.explanation: Modal verbs like 'wollen' (to want to) change the structure of the sentence. The modal verb takes position 2 and is conjugated, while the main verb is pushed to the very end of the clause as an infinitive. 'Ich will verwandeln' is the skeleton → everything else slots in between. The same pattern applies to other modals: können (can), müssen (must), dürfen (may), sollen (should).

grammarPoints.rule: 'Weil' sends the verb to the end of its clause
grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Ich komme wieder, weil ich dich nicht vergesse!
grammarPoints.explanation: 'Weil' (because) is a subordinating conjunction → it introduces a dependent clause where the verb must go to the very end. Compare: 'Ich vergesse dich nicht' (normal order) vs 'weil ich dich nicht vergesse' (verb at end after weil). This verb-final rule applies to all subordinating conjunctions: weil, dass, wenn, obwohl, weil, da.

To express the future in German, use the conjugated form of 'werden' in position 2, with the main verb as an infinitive at the end. 'Du wirst finden' = you will find. Note that Germans often use the present tense instead for near-future events → 'werden + infinitive' adds emphasis or certainty, as the witch uses it here as a threat.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Kasper versteckt sich hinter einem großen Baum.
grammarPoints.explanation: 'Sich verstecken' (to hide oneself) is a reflexive verb → the action reflects back onto the subject. The reflexive pronoun 'sich' is used for the 3rd person (er/sie/es/sie plural). For other persons: ich verstecke mich, du versteckst dich, wir verstecken uns. Many everyday German verbs are reflexive: sich waschen (to wash), sich setzen (to sit down), sich freuen (to be happy).

German has two main negation words. 'Nicht' negates verbs, adjectives, and adverbs ('kommt nicht zurück'). 'Kein' negates nouns directly and must agree with the noun's gender and case → 'keine Angst' (feminine), 'keinen Frosch' (masculine accusative), 'kein Geld' (neuter). Think of 'kein' as 'not a' or 'no': 'Wir haben keine Angst' = 'We have no fear'.

When an adjective follows an indefinite article (ein, eine, einen) in the accusative case, it takes a 'weak' ending. For masculine accusative nouns: 'einen großen Besen' → the article already shows the masculine accusative (-en), so the adjective also takes -en. This pattern → article signals the case, adjective follows with -en → is the most common adjective pattern learners encounter.

grammarPoints.rule: Separable verbs → prefix travels to the end, including with negation
grammarPoints.explanation: Separable verbs split in a main clause: the conjugated base verb stays in position 2, and the prefix goes to the end. When the sentence is also negated, 'nicht' sits just before the prefix: 'kommt nicht zurück' (does not come back). This is the fixed order: base verb → [rest of sentence] → nicht → prefix. Compare: 'kommt zurück' (comes back) vs 'kommt nicht zurück' (does not come back).
